section: Consumer Rights & Business Obligations (as amended by CPRA)
description: The California Consumer Privacy Act (as substantially amended and expanded by the California Privacy Rights Act, CPRA) — codified at California Civil Code § 1798.100 et seq. A U.S. state privacy law, not a federal one, enforced by the California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) and the state Attorney General.

source.label: California Legislative Information — Civil Code § 1798.100 et seq. (CCPA)

items: CCPA — the California Consumer Privacy Act (2018), substantially amended and expanded by the **California Privacy Rights Act (CPRA)**, effective January 1, 2023.

A **California state law**, not federal — codified at California Civil Code § 1798.100 et seq. Applies based on doing business in California and meeting revenue/data-volume thresholds, not on where your company is headquartered.
Enforced by the **California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA)** — the first dedicated U.S. state privacy regulator — plus the California Attorney General.

overview.title: Key Differences from Other Compliance Frameworks

blocks.text: CCPA vs GDPR

items: **CCPA** — opt-out model by default for most data uses (you can sell/share data until a consumer opts out), with California residents specifically in scope.
**GDPR** — opt-in model by default (you generally need a legal basis, often consent, before processing), with anyone in the EU in scope regardless of nationality.

blocks.text: See the [GDPR page](/frameworks/gdpr) for the EU's opt-in model side by side with CCPA's opt-out default — this is the single biggest structural difference between the two.
CCPA vs HIPAA

items: **CCPA** — general-purpose consumer privacy law covering most personal information, with broad business-facing obligations (notice, rights fulfillment, vendor contracts).
**HIPAA** — narrowly scoped to protected health information (PHI) specifically, with its own separate rights and safeguards model. CCPA explicitly exempts information already covered by HIPAA.

blocks.text: "Sale" and "sharing" under CCPA are defined broadly enough to capture things many companies don't think of as selling data — including some forms of ad tech and cross-context behavioral advertising. Don't assume "we don't sell data" without checking the statutory definition.

items: **Consumer Rights** — the specific rights California residents have over their personal information: know, delete, correct, opt-out of sale/sharing, limit use of sensitive data, and non-discrimination.

**Business Obligations & Enforcement** — the operational requirements on businesses: notice, timelines for responding to requests, definitions, and how the law is actually enforced (private right of action for breaches, administrative penalties, CPPA rulemaking).

blocks.text: CCPA/CPRA doesn't use "Articles" like GDPR — it's organized as sequential Civil Code sections (§ 1798.100, § 1798.105, etc.), each covering one specific right or obligation.
See the [Controls tab](/frameworks/ccpa/controls) for the specific Civil Code sections, with plain-English notes on what each actually requires.
It's Not Optional (If You're In Scope)

items: **Legal requirement** — if you meet the applicability thresholds (revenue, data volume, or revenue from selling personal information), compliance is mandatory, not optional.

**Private right of action for breaches** — unlike most of CCPA (which is enforced by the CPPA/AG), a data breach specifically exposes you to direct consumer lawsuits with statutory damages of $100-$750 per consumer per incident.
**Administrative penalties** — the CPPA can levy fines per violation, with intentional violations and violations involving minors' data penalized more heavily.

blocks.text: Business Reasons Beyond the Legal Floor

CCPA/CPRA has become a de facto national baseline for many U.S. companies, since building separate privacy programs per state is rarely practical — a solid CCPA program covers a meaningful chunk of what other state privacy laws (Virginia, Colorado, and others) require too.
Quick self-score across both categories. If you're answering "no" to more than half in either, prioritize it.

rows: Can you disclose what personal information you've collected about a specific consumer on request?
Can you actually delete a consumer's data on request, including from vendors?
Can you correct inaccurate personal information a consumer identifies?
Do you have a working "Do Not Sell or Share" mechanism if you sell/share data?
Can consumers limit use of sensitive personal information (if you process any)?
Do you avoid penalizing consumers (price, service level) for exercising their rights?
Does your privacy notice disclose categories collected, purposes, and retention?
Can you verify and respond to a consumer request within the required timeframe?
Do you have a "Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information" link if required?
Do your contracts with service providers include CCPA-required data protection terms?
Do you maintain reasonable security procedures appropriate to the data you hold?

blocks.text: Identifying Gaps

items: A data inventory/mapping exercise — similar to GDPR's RoPA — is the fastest way to surface where CCPA actually applies in your systems.
[The CPPA's official regulations](https://cppa.ca.gov/regulations/) are the canonical practical guidance beyond the statute text itself.

blocks.text: What Takes Longest

Operationalizing the right to delete consistently takes the longest — the same challenge as GDPR's erasure right — because deletion has to actually propagate through backups, logs, and every downstream vendor you've shared data with, not just your primary database.
What to Implement First

items: **A data inventory** — the foundational step everything else depends on, same as GDPR's RoPA.
**An accurate privacy notice** — required regardless of maturity, and cheap to get right once the inventory exists.
**A working opt-out mechanism** — if you sell or share data, this is one of the most visible/checked requirements.
**Vendor contract updates** — an inventory-and-paperwork exercise, easy to let slip.
Once those are in place, build out full rights-fulfillment (access/delete/correct) with real verification and response-time tracking, since that needs system-level support, not just a policy.

blocks.text: Like GDPR, there's no certification or audit cycle — CCPA compliance is demonstrated on an ongoing basis and tested when a regulator investigates or a consumer sues over a breach.
Step-by-Step Process

items: **Determine applicability** — check whether you meet CCPA's revenue/data-volume thresholds.
**Map your data** — inventory what personal information you collect, why, and where it goes.
**Update your privacy notice** — disclose categories, purposes, retention, and consumer rights.
**Build rights-fulfillment processes** — know, delete, correct, opt-out, limit use of sensitive data.
**Update vendor contracts** — CCPA-required terms with every service provider/contractor touching personal information.
**Implement reasonable security** — appropriate technical and organizational measures, since this is what the private right of action for breaches actually turns on.

blocks.text: CCPA doesn't have a voluntary third-party audit. Enforcement runs through two distinct tracks depending on the violation type.
Two Enforcement Tracks

items: **CPPA/AG administrative enforcement** — covers most CCPA violations (notice failures, rights-fulfillment failures, etc.), with civil penalties per violation.

**Private right of action** — narrowly limited to data breaches involving nonencrypted, nonredacted personal information resulting from a failure to maintain reasonable security. Consumers get 30 days to notify the business, which can cure the violation to avoid statutory damages.

blocks.text: Common Findings

Enforcement actions consistently point to the same gaps: privacy notices that don't match actual data practices, missing or broken opt-out mechanisms, and inadequate security controls that turn an incident into breach litigation exposure.
Compliance is continuous — there's no report to renew annually, but obligations apply to every new data collection practice you introduce.
Ongoing Requirements
Keep the data inventory current, review vendor contracts as you onboard new service providers, and monitor CPPA rulemaking — the regulations have continued evolving materially since CPRA took effect.
Scaling Compliance

New products or features that introduce new categories of personal information (especially sensitive personal information) need to be reflected in your privacy notice and rights-fulfillment processes before launch, not after.

categories.title: Consumer Rights
categories.summary: The specific rights California residents have over their own personal information (§ 1798.100–1798.125).

controls.criteria: General duties of businesses that collect personal information — disclose categories collected and the purposes for collecting them, at or before collection.
controls.notes: Your privacy notice needs to actually name the categories and purposes, reviewed whenever data practices change.

controls.criteria: Right to delete — consumers can request deletion of personal information a business has collected from them, subject to specific statutory exceptions.
controls.notes: The hardest right to operationalize — deletion needs to propagate through backups, logs, and any service providers you've shared data with.

controls.criteria: Right to correct — consumers can request correction of inaccurate personal information, taking into account the nature of the data and purposes of processing.
controls.notes: A straightforward self-service or support-ticket correction mechanism usually satisfies this.

controls.criteria: Right to know / access — consumers can request the categories and specific pieces of personal information a business has collected about them.
controls.notes: The core access-request right — needs a defined verification process and response timeline, similar to GDPR's DSAR.

controls.criteria: Right to know about disclosures — consumers can request disclosure of what personal information is sold or shared, and the categories of third parties receiving it.
controls.notes: Track what you disclose to whom so you can actually answer this request, not just what you collect.

controls.criteria: Right to opt out of sale or sharing — consumers can direct a business not to sell or share their personal information to third parties.
controls.notes: "Sharing" is defined broadly enough to include some cross-context behavioral advertising — don't assume this doesn't apply just because you don't call it a "sale."

controls.criteria: Right to limit use of sensitive personal information — consumers can direct a business to limit use of sensitive personal information to what's necessary to perform the requested services.
controls.notes: Only relevant if you process sensitive personal information (as CCPA defines it — precise ID numbers, precise geolocation, health data, and more) — but if you do, this needs its own opt-out mechanism.

controls.criteria: Non-discrimination — businesses cannot deny goods/services, charge different prices, or provide a different level of service because a consumer exercised a CCPA right.
controls.notes: Financial incentive programs are allowed but must be reasonably related to the value of the data, and disclosed transparently — don't structure opt-outs to functionally punish consumers.

categories.title: Business Obligations & Enforcement
categories.summary: The operational requirements on businesses, and how the law is actually enforced (§ 1798.130–1798.185).

controls.criteria: Notice, disclosure, correction, and deletion requirements — specific methods and timelines businesses must follow to receive and respond to consumer requests.
controls.notes: Response timelines are statutory, not discretionary — build a defined SLA and verification process rather than handling requests ad hoc.

controls.criteria: Requirement to post a "Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information" (and, where applicable, "Limit the Use of My Sensitive Personal Information") link on the homepage.
controls.notes: One of the most visible, easily-checked CCPA requirements — a broken or missing opt-out link is a common, avoidable finding.

controls.criteria: Definitions — including "personal information," "sensitive personal information," "sale," "share," "business," and the applicability thresholds.
controls.notes: Read this section carefully before assuming CCPA doesn't apply to you or that a given activity doesn't count as a "sale"/"share" — the definitions are broader than plain-English intuition suggests.

controls.criteria: Private right of action — consumers whose nonencrypted, nonredacted personal information is breached due to a business's failure to maintain reasonable security can sue for statutory damages of $100–$750 per consumer per incident, or actual damages.
controls.notes: This is the one part of CCPA enforced directly by consumers rather than the regulator — reasonable security isn't optional risk-management advice, it's what stands between an incident and class-action-scale liability.

controls.criteria: Administrative enforcement — the CPPA can investigate and levy civil penalties per violation, with higher penalties for intentional violations and violations involving minors' data.
controls.notes: Covers essentially all other CCPA violations beyond the breach-specific private right of action — notice failures, rights-fulfillment failures, and more.

controls.criteria: Rulemaking authority — the California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) has authority to adopt regulations implementing and interpreting CCPA.
controls.notes: CCPA compliance isn't just the statute text — CPPA's actual regulations (which continue to evolve) fill in significant operational detail the statute leaves open.
